Yeah. The human kind of fear of running out of stuff is very understandable and it happens quite a lot. We got really worried back in the kind of 16th century. We haven't run out of copper yet. Pretty much everything you touch on a daily basis will have had some inputs. But it's also Korea. It's also, for instance, the UK. It used to make the nibs for pens, you know. so the the fountain pen nibs but now they make little metal parts and they're very good at pressing those parts they make an electrode there which goes into the rear view mirror of a car and it does the auto dimming so you know when someone flashes their headlight behind you and it does the auto dimming this little factory in birmingham makes this little electrode they make more than 50 of the world's supply of those little electrodes one factory in birmingham that that no one has ever heard of. There are millions of examples of that, of little tiny components made in a factory that you've never heard of in a country. You know, this is the UK where we don't make much stuff anymore. But it could be in Korea, it could be, you know, in Taiwan, it could be in Singapore, it could be anywhere. If you think about kind of semiconductors, you know, America would like to be able to make all of its semiconductors itself. And that's, I can understand why. China would like to be able to make all of its computer chips itself. But it's just impossible. it is just impossible. If you think about the machines that you use here in Korea to make those incredibly small, you know, two nanometer chips, those machines are made by a company in the Netherlands, ASML. So you're already reliant on a company in the Netherlands. That company in the Netherlands is reliant on thousands of other companies which provide the components that go inside their machines. So the world is this complex web of interdependencies. And part of the product of that world is we have all the stuff we want and we know that we can order it and we know that it turns up. But I understand, you know, the politics of the period we're living through right now and it's a scary period. You know, it's a new Cold War at the very least, possibly even worse than that. And so it's understandable that people are looking at, are talking about independence. There's a massive gap between how the world actually is right now and the world that they perceive it to be. But I hope that policymakers try to understand the material world, as I call it, the complexity of the devices they use and all of the products that they use before they start to take any kind of rash moves. Because what this shows is how reliant we are on each other. Thank you very much. Thank you.",
